chat_messages.text:
Looks like the whole command got pasted into your `.zshrc` instead of just the export. Fix line 5:

```bash
sed -i '' '5s/.*/export DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH="\/opt\/homebrew\/opt\/expat\/lib:$DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH"/' ~/.zshrc
```

Then:

```bash
source ~/.zshrc
python3 -m venv venv
```

Line 5 should now be just `export DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/expat/lib:$DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH"` — without the `python3 -m venv venv` part.

The venv is working now — this is a different issue. Matplotlib 3.9.0 is too old to compile on Python 3.14 + your newer Apple Clang. The freetype bundled with matplotlib 3.9.0 has C code that doesn't compile with Clang 21.

The fix depends on whether you can change the version. What does your `requirements.txt` look like? Specifically, do you *need* matplotlib 3.9.0, or can you use a newer version? Matplotlib 3.10+ should work with Python 3.14.

If you can bump it:

```bash
pip install matplotlib --upgrade
```

or change `matplotlib==3.9.0` to `matplotlib>=3.10` in your `requirements.txt`.

If you're locked to 3.9.0 (e.g. for a class or project), then you'd need to downgrade Python instead — `brew install python@3.12` and create your venv with `python3.12 -m venv venv`. Matplotlib 3.9.0 simply won't build against Python 3.14.

or change `matplotlib==3.9.0` to `matplotlib>=3.10` in your `requirements.txt`.

If you're locked to 3.9.0 (e.g. for a class or project), then you'd need to downgrade Python instead — `brew install python@3.12` and create your venv with `python3.12 -m venv venv`. Matplotlib 3.9.0 simply won't build against Python 3.14.

chat_messages.text:
Your editor (likely VS Code) is looking for `.venv` but your virtual environment is called `venv`. You need to point it to the right one.

In VS Code, open settings (`Cmd + ,`), search for `python.defaultInterpreterPath`, and set it to:

```
./venv/bin/python
```

Or use the command palette (`Cmd + Shift + P`) → "Python: Select Interpreter" → choose the one under `venv/bin/python`.

chat_messages.text:
The expat issue is back — the `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` fix only applied to your terminal session, but VS Code/Jupyter launches Python in its own environment where that variable isn't set.

To fix this permanently, you need `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` in your `.zshrc` so every process picks it up. Let's verify it's actually there correctly:

```bash
grep DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH ~/.zshrc
```

If it's there, the problem is that VS Code doesn't always source `.zshrc`. You'll need to also add it to `~/.zprofile`:

```bash
echo 'export DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/expat/lib:$DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH"' >> ~/.zprofile
```

Then **fully quit VS Code** (not just close the window — `Cmd + Q`) and reopen it. VS Code needs a full restart to pick up environment changes.

Honestly though, you're going to keep hitting Python 3.14 compatibility issues (the matplotlib build failure, this expat problem, potentially others). If this is for a class or project and you just need things to work, I'd strongly recommend switching to Python 3.12 or 3.13:

```bash
brew install python@3.13
python3.13 -m venv venv
```

That'll save you a lot of headaches. Python 3.14 is very new and the ecosystem hasn't fully caught up yet.

Honestly though, you're going to keep hitting Python 3.14 compatibility issues (the matplotlib build failure, this expat problem, potentially others). If this is for a class or project and you just need things to work, I'd strongly recommend switching to Python 3.12 or 3.13:

chat_messages.text:
At this point, the `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` approach isn't reliable — VS Code and Jupyter can ignore it depending on how they launch processes.

Let's try the more direct fix. Force-link Homebrew's expat so it actually replaces the system one in Homebrew's path:

```bash
brew link expat --force
```

If that works, restart VS Code (`Cmd + Q`, reopen) and try the kernel again.

If `brew link --force` refuses, there's one more option — directly point the Python dylib at the right expat:

```bash
install_name_tool -change /usr/lib/libexpat.1.dylib /opt/homebrew/opt/expat/lib/libexpat.1.dylib /opt/homebrew/Cellar/python@3.14/3.14.5/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.14/lib/python3.14/lib-dynload/pyexpat.cpython-314-darwin.so
```

This permanently rewires `pyexpat.so` to load Homebrew's expat instead of the system one. No environment variables needed.

But seriously — if you're not specifically required to use 3.14, switching to `python@3.13` would eliminate all of these issues at once.

content.text:
At this point, the `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` approach isn't reliable — VS Code and Jupyter can ignore it depending on how they launch processes.

Let's try the more direct fix. Force-link Homebrew's expat so it actually replaces the system one in Homebrew's path:

```bash
brew link expat --force
```

If that works, restart VS Code (`Cmd + Q`, reopen) and try the kernel again.

If `brew link --force` refuses, there's one more option — directly point the Python dylib at the right expat:

```bash
install_name_tool -change /usr/lib/libexpat.1.dylib /opt/homebrew/opt/expat/lib/libexpat.1.dylib /opt/homebrew/Cellar/python@3.14/3.14.5/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.14/lib/python3.14/lib-dynload/pyexpat.cpython-314-darwin.so
```

This permanently rewires `pyexpat.so` to load Homebrew's expat instead of the system one. No environment variables needed.

But seriously — if you're not specifically required to use 3.14, switching to `python@3.13` would eliminate all of these issues at once.

chat_messages.text:
That explains everything. You're on macOS 26.2 (Tahoe) and `/usr/lib/libexpat.1.dylib` doesn't even exist on disk — Apple moved system libraries into a shared cache. But the Homebrew bottles were built expecting it there.

Reinstall from source:

```bash
brew reinstall python@3.13 --build-from-source
```

Then recreate your venv:

```bash
rm -rf venv
python3.13 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

Building from source will compile `pyexpat` against Homebrew's expat (which is actually on disk), rather than relying on a system library that no longer exists in the traditional location.

name: SSH access to Genesys 2 FPGA
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person asked about remotely accessing a Genesys 2 FPGA (Xilinx Kintex-7 XC7K325T) board. They initially inquired about SSH access, and Claude explained that this would require running a soft-core processor (MicroBlaze or RISC-V) with Linux on the FPGA fabric, involving PetaLinux, AXI Ethernet IP cores, and DDR3 memory. However, the person clarified their actual goal was simply remote programming, not running Linux on the FPGA.

Claude then provided the more appropriate solution: using Vivado's `hw_server` for remote programming. The recommended approach involves running `hw_server -s tcp::3121` on the machine physically connected to the board via JTAG/USB, then connecting to it from a remote Vivado instance via Hardware Manager using the host machine's IP and port 3121. Claude also covered securing the connection by tunneling port 3121 over SSH (`ssh -L 3121:localhost:3121 user@host-machine-ip`) and scripting remote programming via Vivado's Tcl interface, including the key commands: `open_hw_manager`, `connect_hw_server`, `open_hw_target`, setting `PROGRAM.FILE`, and `program_hw_devices`. The person's need was straightforward remote bitstream programming without any FPGA-side Linux setup.

chat_messages.text: is it possible to ssh into the Genesys 2 FPGA

content.text: is it possible to ssh into the Genesys 2 FPGA

chat_messages.text:
Yes, it's possible to SSH into a Genesys 2 FPGA board, but it requires some setup since the board itself doesn't natively run an SSH server — you need to have a soft processor system running Linux on the FPGA fabric.

Here's what's typically involved:

**The general approach** is to synthesize a MicroBlaze or RISC-V soft-core processor design in Vivado, boot Linux (usually PetaLinux) on that soft core, and configure networking over the board's Ethernet port. Once Linux is running with networking enabled, you can run an SSH daemon (like Dropbear or OpenSSH) and SSH in like any other Linux box.

**Key requirements:**

The Genesys 2 (Xilinx Kintex-7 XC7K325T) has a Gigabit Ethernet PHY, which makes networking straightforward once you have the right IP core (Xilinx AXI Ethernet or TEMAC). You also need enough block RAM or DDR3 (the board has 1 GB) to run Linux.

**Typical workflow:**

You'd create a block design in Vivado with a MicroBlaze processor, AXI Ethernet (connected to the on-board Marvell 88E1510 PHY), DDR3 memory controller, UART, and a timer. Then use PetaLinux to build a Linux image targeting that hardware, configure networking (static IP or DHCP), enable an SSH server in the root filesystem, and boot it up.

**Alternatively**, if you just want remote access to program/debug the FPGA (not run Linux on it), you can use Vivado's remote hardware server (`hw_server`) — run it on a host PC connected to the board via JTAG, and connect to it remotely from another Vivado instance. That's not SSH into the FPGA itself, but it gives you remote programming and debugging.

Digilent has reference designs and documentation for the Genesys 2 that can serve as a starting point for the Linux-on-MicroBlaze approach. Would you like more detail on any of these paths?

chat_messages.text: yeah i just need to program it remotely

content.text: yeah i just need to program it remotely

chat_messages.text:
For remote programming, the Vivado Hardware Server approach is the simplest path — no Linux-on-FPGA needed.

**On the machine physically connected to the Genesys 2 via JTAG/USB:**

Run the hardware server:

```
hw_server -stcp::3121
```

This listens on port 3121 by default. The machine just needs Vivado Lab Edition (free) or full Vivado installed.

**From your remote machine:**

In Vivado's Hardware Manager, instead of "Open Target → Auto Connect," use "Open Target → Open New Target" and enter the IP address of the host machine and port 3121. You'll see the FPGA as if it were locally connected and can program it with your bitstream.

**A few practical notes:**

You'll want to make sure port 3121 is open/forwarded on the host machine. If you're on the same LAN that's usually straightforward; over the internet you'd want to tunnel it through SSH to the host PC for security:

```
ssh -L 3121:localhost:3121 user@host-machine-ip
```

Then connect Vivado to `localhost:3121` on your local machine.

You can also script programming remotely using Vivado's Tcl interface:

```tcl
open_hw_manager
connect_hw_server -url remote_host_ip:3121
open_hw_target
set_property PROGRAM.FILE {path/to/design.bit} [current_hw_device]
program_hw_devices
```

This is really all there is to it. The host PC stays on with `hw_server` running, and you can program the board from anywhere.

name: Memory-safe CVA6 processor implementation
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person is a student working on a computer architecture assignment involving an implementation of the CVA6 processor modified for memory safety. They had no prior knowledge of CVA6 or the surrounding concepts and needed foundational explanations before understanding their specific assignment. The conversation covered two main educational topics: first, an explanation of CVA6 and the Capstone-RISC-V capability-based security extension; second, an explanation of MMUs, page tables, and page table walkers in the context of their assignment.

Claude explained that CVA6 is an open-source RISC-V processor core maintained by the OpenHW Group, and that the Capstone extension modifies it to enforce memory safety at the hardware level using capabilities — unforgeable 128-bit tokens encoding bounds, permissions, and type that replace raw memory pointers. Key Capstone concepts explained included capability types (linear, non-linear, revocation, uninitialised, sealed), domains as isolated execution compartments, C-mode as a capability-enforcing privilege level, and new instructions like MOVC, LDC/STC, SPLIT, SHRINK, SEAL, CALL, and RETURN.

For the assignment question, Claude explained that an MMU translates virtual addresses to physical addresses using a multi-level page table structure (Sv39 for RV64), and that a page table walker is the hardware unit performing this multi-step memory lookup. The assignment — implementing a page table walker in the MMU for the Capstone processor — was explained as requiring the standard RISC-V multi-level page table walk logic integrated with Capstone's capability checking framework, specifically the cpmp registers and offsetmmu register, so that physical memory accesses during the walk are validated against capability bounds and permissions.

Great, now I have the full picture. Let me explain this to you.

## What is CVA6?

CVA6 is an open-source RISC-V processor core that can be configured as either a 32-bit or 64-bit CPU. It was originally created by ETH Zürich and the University of Bologna (under the name "ARIANE") and is now maintained by the OpenHW Group. Think of it like an open-source Intel or ARM processor design — it can run Linux, FreeRTOS, and Zephyr, and it targets both FPGA prototyping and real ASIC chips. It implements the standard RISC-V instruction set (specifically RV64I with the Zicsr extension, in the configuration relevant to your document).

## What is the Capstone-RISC-V document about?

The PDF you uploaded describes **Capstone**, an extension to the RISC-V instruction set that adds **capability-based security** directly into the hardware. Rather than being a separate chip, it modifies the CVA6 processor's design so that memory safety is enforced by the CPU itself, not just by software.

## The core problem Capstone solves

In a normal processor, any piece of code with enough privilege (like an OS kernel) can read or write *any* memory location. A buffer overflow bug in one program can corrupt another program's data. The traditional privilege model (user mode → kernel → hypervisor) is rigid and coarse-grained.

Capstone replaces raw memory addresses with **capabilities** — unforgeable tokens that encode *what* memory you can access, *how* (read/write/execute), and *which range*. You can think of a capability as a pointer that carries its own access-control policy and that the hardware enforces. You can't fabricate one from thin air, you can't widen its permissions, and you can't extend its bounds. You can only receive one from something that already had it and narrow it down.

## Key concepts

**Capabilities (128 bits):** Each capability contains a cursor (current address), base and end (bounds), permissions (read/write/execute combinations), a validity bit, and a type field. Registers are widened from 64 to 129 bits to hold either a regular integer or a capability. The hardware tracks which type each register contains and forbids confusion between them.

**Capability types:** This is where Capstone gets interesting — it defines several types:

- **Linear** — the default. A linear capability is guaranteed to exist in only one place at a time. You can move it between registers, but not copy it. This ensures exclusive access to a memory region.
- **Non-linear** — can be freely copied (like a shared read-only reference). Created by "delinearizing" a linear capability via the DELIN instruction.
- **Revocation** — can't access memory directly, but conveys the authority to *invalidate* other capabilities derived from the same region. Created from a linear capability via MREV, then used with REVOKE.
- **Uninitialised** — write-only. You must write to every byte in the region before you can "upgrade" it to a linear capability via INIT. This prevents leaking stale secrets from previously-used memory.
- **Sealed / Sealed-return** — used for domain crossing (essentially secure function calls between isolated compartments). A sealed capability can't be directly used; it must be "called into" with the CALL instruction, which triggers a safe context switch.

**Domains:** When capability mode is active, the system organizes execution into *domains* — isolated compartments each with their own register state. Domains interact through CALL/RETURN instructions that atomically swap context, preventing one domain from seeing another's registers or memory. This replaces the traditional fixed hypervisor → kernel → user hierarchy with a dynamic, software-defined one.

**C-mode:** The Caplifive extension adds "Capability mode" (C-mode), which replaces M-mode (machine mode, the highest privilege in standard RISC-V) when enabled. In C-mode, *all* memory accesses — including instruction fetches — must go through capabilities. Lower privilege levels (S-mode, U-mode) can still use traditional virtual memory, but their physical memory access is ultimately backed by capabilities configured in a modified PMP (Physical Memory Protection) structure.

## How instructions change

Capstone adds about 20 new instructions (all using the "custom-2" opcode space, so they don't conflict with standard RISC-V), including MOVC (move capability), LDC/STC (load/store capability), SPLIT (divide a capability's range into two), SHRINK (narrow bounds), TIGHTEN (reduce permissions), SEAL, CALL, RETURN, REVOKE, and others.

Existing RISC-V instructions like `ld`, `sd`, `beq`, `jal` are adjusted so that when running in C-mode, the address register is interpreted as a capability, and bounds/permissions are checked before every memory access.

The program counter itself becomes a capability, so even instruction fetch is bounds-checked — code can only execute within the range its PC capability allows.

## Why this matters

The net effect is that the hardware itself prevents entire classes of vulnerabilities: buffer overflows can't escape capability bounds, use-after-free is caught by revocation, privilege escalation is blocked because you can't forge wider capabilities, and compartment isolation doesn't depend on trusting the OS kernel. All of this happens at near-zero performance overhead compared to software-only approaches, because it's baked into the instruction set and enforced by the CPU pipeline.

In short: this document describes how to take a standard open-source RISC-V processor (CVA6) and turn it into a capability machine where memory safety is a hardware guarantee rather than a software hope.

# Introduction[](#introduction "Link to this heading")

## License[](#license "Link to this heading")

Copyright 2023 OpenHW Group and Thales

SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0 WITH SHL-2.1

Licensed under the Solderpad Hardware License v 2.1 (the “License”); you may not use this file except in compliance with the License, or, at your option, the Apache License version 2.0.
You may obtain a copy of the License at <https://solderpad.org/licenses/SHL-2.1/>.
Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, any work distributed under the License is distributed on an “AS IS” BASIS, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and limitations under the License.

## Work In Progress[](#work-in-progress "Link to this heading")

This document is a work in progress and the team currently drafting it focuses on its use to verify several configurations of CVA6.

The current limitation of documentation on CVA6 is well understood.
Rather than regretting this, the reader is encouraged to contribute to it to make CVA6 an even better core.
To contribute to the project, refer to the [Contributing](https://github.com/jquevremont/cva6/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md) guidelines and get in touch with the team.

## Target Audience[](#target-audience "Link to this heading")

The CVA6 user manual targets:

- SW programmers

- HW designers who integrate CVA6 into a SoC/ASIC/FPGA

- Architects who design a coprocessor for the CV-X-IF interface and who need to create SW to use it

- HW designers who synthetize/place&route/verify a design that embeds CVA6

- Verification engineers involved in the OpenHW Group’s CVA6 project who use this manual as a reference.

The user guide does not target people who dig into CVA6 design. No internal mechanisms are described here,
except if the user has some sort of control on it. A separate design document digs into the core microarchitecture.

## CVA6 Overview[](#cva6-overview "Link to this heading")

**CVA6** is a RISC-V compatible application processor core that can be configured
as a 32- or 64-bit core: **CV32A6** and **CV64A6**.

CVA6 can be configured to the users’ and application needs thanks to several
parameters and optional features (MMU, PMP, FPU, cache organization and size…).
It targets **FPGA** and **ASIC** technologies.

CVA6, as an application core, can run many operating systems. It has already been
demonstrated with embedded **Linux** distributions (built with **BuildRoot** and **Yocto**), **FreeRTOS** and **Zephyr**.

CVA6 features the **CV-X-IF** coprocessor interface to extend the set of instructions it can execute.

The goal of CVA6 is to be **fully compliant** with RISC-V specifications and feature no or extremely
few custom extensions (except through extensions on CV-X-IF interface).

CV32A6 and CV64A6 share the same **SystemVerilog** source code, available in this [GitHub](https://github.com/openhwgroup/cva6/) repository.

CV64A6 is an industrial evolution of ARIANE created by ETH Zürich and the
University of Bologna. CV32A6 is a later addition by Thales. CVA6 is now
curated at the OpenHW Group by its members.

## Configurations[](#configurations "Link to this heading")

CVA6 is actually a family of cores, as CVA6 can be configured to the users’ needs with more than 50 parameters.
A configuration is defined as a given set of parameters.

A few configurations undergo a complete verification process to bring them to **TRL-5**,
the maturity level where they can be integrated in production ASICs.

This manual includes generic descriptions of CVA6 capabilities, as well as their applicability to
the verified configurations.

As of today, two configurations are being verified and addressed in this document:

| Configuration | Short description | Target | Privilege levels | Supported RISC-V ISA | CV-X-IF |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **CV32A60AX** | 32-bit **application** core | ASIC | Machine, Supervisor, User | RV32IMACZicsr_Zifencei_Zicount_Zba_Zbb_Zbc_Zbs_Zcb_Zicond | Included |
| **CV32A60X** | 32-bit **embedded** core | ASIC | Machine only | RV32IMCZicsr_Zifencei_Zba_Zbb_Zbc_Zbs_Zcb | Included |
| **CV64A6_MMU** | 64-bit **embedded** core with MMU | ASIC | Machine, Supervisor, User | RV64IMCZicsr_Zifencei_Zba_Zbb_Zbc_Zbs_Zcb | Included |


CV32A60X is an interim part number until the team can decide if this configuration is single- or dual-issue.
If the dual-issue architecture is selected, the part number will become CV32A65X to denote the extra performance.

In the future, dedicated user manuals for each configuration could be generated. The team is looking for a contributor to implement this through *templating*.

## Scope of the IP[](#scope-of-the-ip "Link to this heading")

The **scope of the IP** refers the subsystem that is documented here.

![../_images/cva6_scope.png](https://docs.openhwgroup.org/projects/cva6-user-manual/_images/cva6_scope.png)

As displayed in the picture above, the IP comprises:

- The CVA6 core;

- L1 write-through cache;

- Optional FPU;

- Optional MMU;

- Optional PMP;

- CSR;

- Performance counters;

- AXI interface;

- Interface with the P-Mesh coherence system of OpenPiton;

- CV-X-IF coprocessor interface (not shown).

These are not part of the IP (several solutions can be used):

- CLINT or PLIC Interrupt modules;

- Debug module (such as DTM);

- Support of L1 write-back cache.

## Specifications and References[](#specifications-and-references "Link to this heading")

### Applicable Specifications[](#applicable-specifications "Link to this heading")

CVA6 strives to comply with the following specifications. When the
specifications allow variations (parameters, optional features…),
this users’ guide will detail them.

[RVunpriv]

“The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume I: User-Level ISA,
Document Version 20191213”, Editors Andrew Waterman and Krste Asanović,
RISC-V Foundation, December 13, 2019.

[RVpriv]

“The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume II: Privileged
Architecture, Document Version 20211203”, Editors Andrew Waterman, Krste
Asanović and John Hauser, RISC-V Foundation, December 4, 2021.

[RVdbg]

“RISC-V External Debug Support, Document Version 0.13.2”,
Editors Tim Newsome and Megan Wachs, RISC-V Foundation, March 22, 2019.

[RVcompat]

“RISC-V Architectural Compatibility Test Framework”, <https://github.com/riscv-non-isa/riscv-arch-test>.

[AXI]

AXI Specification, <https://developer.arm.com/documentation/ihi0022/hc>.

[CV-X-IF]

CV-X-IF coprocessor interface currently
prepared at OpenHW Group; current version in <https://docs.openhwgroup.org/projects/openhw-group-core-v-xif/>.

[OpenPiton]

“OpenPiton Microarchitecture Specification”, Princeton
University, <https://parallel.princeton.edu/openpiton/docs/micro_arch.pdf>.

Great, now I have the full picture. Let me explain this to you.

## What is CVA6?

CVA6 is an open-source RISC-V processor core that can be configured as either a 32-bit or 64-bit CPU. It was originally created by ETH Zürich and the University of Bologna (under the name "ARIANE") and is now maintained by the OpenHW Group. Think of it like an open-source Intel or ARM processor design — it can run Linux, FreeRTOS, and Zephyr, and it targets both FPGA prototyping and real ASIC chips. It implements the standard RISC-V instruction set (specifically RV64I with the Zicsr extension, in the configuration relevant to your document).

## What is the Capstone-RISC-V document about?

The PDF you uploaded describes **Capstone**, an extension to the RISC-V instruction set that adds **capability-based security** directly into the hardware. Rather than being a separate chip, it modifies the CVA6 processor's design so that memory safety is enforced by the CPU itself, not just by software.

## The core problem Capstone solves

In a normal processor, any piece of code with enough privilege (like an OS kernel) can read or write *any* memory location. A buffer overflow bug in one program can corrupt another program's data. The traditional privilege model (user mode → kernel → hypervisor) is rigid and coarse-grained.

Capstone replaces raw memory addresses with **capabilities** — unforgeable tokens that encode *what* memory you can access, *how* (read/write/execute), and *which range*. You can think of a capability as a pointer that carries its own access-control policy and that the hardware enforces. You can't fabricate one from thin air, you can't widen its permissions, and you can't extend its bounds. You can only receive one from something that already had it and narrow it down.

## Key concepts

**Capabilities (128 bits):** Each capability contains a cursor (current address), base and end (bounds), permissions (read/write/execute combinations), a validity bit, and a type field. Registers are widened from 64 to 129 bits to hold either a regular integer or a capability. The hardware tracks which type each register contains and forbids confusion between them.

**Capability types:** This is where Capstone gets interesting — it defines several types:

- **Linear** — the default. A linear capability is guaranteed to exist in only one place at a time. You can move it between registers, but not copy it. This ensures exclusive access to a memory region.
- **Non-linear** — can be freely copied (like a shared read-only reference). Created by "delinearizing" a linear capability via the DELIN instruction.
- **Revocation** — can't access memory directly, but conveys the authority to *invalidate* other capabilities derived from the same region. Created from a linear capability via MREV, then used with REVOKE.
- **Uninitialised** — write-only. You must write to every byte in the region before you can "upgrade" it to a linear capability via INIT. This prevents leaking stale secrets from previously-used memory.
- **Sealed / Sealed-return** — used for domain crossing (essentially secure function calls between isolated compartments). A sealed capability can't be directly used; it must be "called into" with the CALL instruction, which triggers a safe context switch.

**Domains:** When capability mode is active, the system organizes execution into *domains* — isolated compartments each with their own register state. Domains interact through CALL/RETURN instructions that atomically swap context, preventing one domain from seeing another's registers or memory. This replaces the traditional fixed hypervisor → kernel → user hierarchy with a dynamic, software-defined one.

**C-mode:** The Caplifive extension adds "Capability mode" (C-mode), which replaces M-mode (machine mode, the highest privilege in standard RISC-V) when enabled. In C-mode, *all* memory accesses — including instruction fetches — must go through capabilities. Lower privilege levels (S-mode, U-mode) can still use traditional virtual memory, but their physical memory access is ultimately backed by capabilities configured in a modified PMP (Physical Memory Protection) structure.

## How instructions change

Capstone adds about 20 new instructions (all using the "custom-2" opcode space, so they don't conflict with standard RISC-V), including MOVC (move capability), LDC/STC (load/store capability), SPLIT (divide a capability's range into two), SHRINK (narrow bounds), TIGHTEN (reduce permissions), SEAL, CALL, RETURN, REVOKE, and others.

Existing RISC-V instructions like `ld`, `sd`, `beq`, `jal` are adjusted so that when running in C-mode, the address register is interpreted as a capability, and bounds/permissions are checked before every memory access.

The program counter itself becomes a capability, so even instruction fetch is bounds-checked — code can only execute within the range its PC capability allows.

## Why this matters

The net effect is that the hardware itself prevents entire classes of vulnerabilities: buffer overflows can't escape capability bounds, use-after-free is caught by revocation, privilege escalation is blocked because you can't forge wider capabilities, and compartment isolation doesn't depend on trusting the OS kernel. All of this happens at near-zero performance overhead compared to software-only approaches, because it's baked into the instruction set and enforced by the CPU pipeline.

In short: this document describes how to take a standard open-source RISC-V processor (CVA6) and turn it into a capability machine where memory safety is a hardware guarantee rather than a software hope.

chat_messages.text: so if this is the assignment "implementation of a page table walker in MMU for our capstone processor" what would that mean? what is a MMU and what would a page table walker do?

content.text: so if this is the assignment "implementation of a page table walker in MMU for our capstone processor" what would that mean? what is a MMU and what would a page table walker do?

chat_messages.text:
Great question — let me build this up from the ground layer.

## What is an MMU?

An **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** is a piece of hardware inside a processor that translates **virtual addresses** into **physical addresses**.

Without an MMU, when your program says "read memory at address 0x1000", the CPU goes directly to physical memory location 0x1000. Every program would see the same memory, and one buggy program could trash another's data.

With an MMU, each program gets its own **virtual address space**. Program A and Program B can both think they're using address 0x1000, but the MMU silently redirects them to different physical locations — say 0x50000 and 0x82000. The OS sets up these mappings, and the hardware enforces them transparently.

## What is a page table?

The mapping from virtual to physical addresses is stored in a data structure in memory called a **page table**. Memory is divided into fixed-size chunks called **pages** (typically 4 KB). The page table is essentially a giant lookup table: "virtual page 7 → physical page 42, with read+write permissions."

In practice, a flat table would be enormous, so RISC-V (and most architectures) use a **multi-level** page table — a tree structure. For RV64, the Sv39 scheme uses 3 levels of tables. To translate one virtual address, the CPU must walk through up to 3 levels of tables stored in memory, reading one entry at each level to find the next, until it arrives at the final physical address.

## What is a page table walker?

A **page table walker (PTW)** is the hardware unit inside the MMU that performs this multi-step lookup automatically. When the CPU encounters a virtual address that isn't already cached in the TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small fast cache of recent translations), the page table walker takes over and does the following:

1. Takes the virtual address and the root page table pointer (from the `satp` register)
2. Reads the first-level page table entry from memory
3. Uses that to find and read the second-level entry
4. Uses that to find and read the third-level entry
5. Extracts the final physical address and permissions
6. Delivers the result back to the CPU (and caches it in the TLB)

Each of those steps is a real memory read, so it's relatively slow — that's why the TLB exists to cache results and avoid walking the table every time.

## What does this mean for your Capstone processor?

This is where it gets interesting. In the Capstone-RISC-V architecture from your document, the system has **C-mode** (capability mode) at the top, and it can host traditional S-mode/U-mode software underneath via the Caplifive extension. That means you can run a standard OS with virtual memory inside a Capstone domain.

But there's a critical twist: in Capstone, **all physical memory access must go through capabilities**. The document describes `cpmp0-15` registers — capability-backed PMP entries — and an `offsetmmu` register that adds an offset to translate logical addresses to physical ones.

So your page table walker can't just produce a physical address and go read memory with it like a normal MMU would. It needs to work within the capability framework. Specifically:

- The PTW needs to validate that the physical addresses it's accessing (to read page table entries) are **within the bounds of a valid capability** configured in the cpmp registers
- The final translated physical address also needs to be checked against these capability-backed PMP entries before the actual data access proceeds
- The permissions from the page table (read/write/execute) must be consistent with what the governing capability allows

In practical terms, your assignment likely involves designing the RTL (hardware description) for a page table walker module that performs the standard multi-level RISC-V page table walk, but integrates with the Capstone capability checking logic — ensuring that the traditional virtual memory mechanism and the capability-based access control work together correctly. The walker would sit inside the MMU, receive translation requests from the CPU pipeline, perform the walk by issuing memory reads (subject to capability checks), and return the translated address along with permission information.

name: Wireshark packet capture analysis
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person shared a Wireshark packet capture file (test-capture.pcapng) and asked Claude to analyze it for anything interesting. Claude used scripted Python analysis with the Scapy library to examine the capture, identifying it as an AirDrop session between Apple devices on a local network. The capture contained 78 packets over approximately 3.75 minutes, consisting entirely of IPv6 link-local traffic with no internet connectivity. Claude identified three devices involved, mDNS/Bonjour service discovery traffic advertising services including `_airdrop._tcp.local` and `_airplay-p2p._tcp.local`, and a TLS 1.3 encrypted file transfer between ports 64114 and 57492. Notable details included a device name suggesting a German-language iPhone ("iPhone von"), a visible AirDrop authentication token (`607acdd2439e`), and a few retransmitted packets indicating minor network hiccups. Claude assessed the capture as benign and consistent with normal Apple ecosystem behavior.

The person then asked an unrelated follow-up question about how card payments work on airplanes without internet connectivity. Claude explained the store-and-forward transaction model used by airlines, where payment data is captured locally and batch-processed after landing. Claude also described supporting mechanisms including offline EMV chip authorization, locally cached card blocklists downloaded pre-flight, and noted that some newer aircraft use satellite internet for real-time processing. The conversation was exploratory and informational with no tasks requiring file access or tools beyond the initial packet analysis.

Here's what's going on in this capture — it's an **AirDrop session** between Apple devices on a local network. Here's the breakdown:

**The basics:** 78 packets over about 3.75 minutes, entirely IPv6 over link-local addresses. No internet traffic at all — this is purely device-to-device communication.

**What's happening:**

1. **mDNS service discovery (53% of traffic):** 41 packets are multicast DNS (port 5353, destination `ff02::fb`) — this is Bonjour/mDNS, Apple's local service discovery protocol. The devices are advertising and querying for services like `_airdrop._tcp.local`, `_airplay-p2p._tcp.local`, `_raop._tcp.local`, and `_applicationServicePairing._tcp.local`.

2. **An AirDrop transfer:** Two AirDrop-capable devices discover each other. One device advertises itself as instance `42cb7bc076b6`, another as `f078b69be0bc`. There's also an **iPhone** involved — its service name shows up as **"iPhone von "** (which is German for "iPhone of", with the owner's name likely truncated or stripped). Its pairing session ID is `CA3F2AFD-677A-4919-881B-587416C66F51`.

3. **TLS handshake + encrypted transfer:** The 37 TCP packets (ports 64114 ↔ 57492) show a TLS 1.3 handshake (`\x16\x03\x01` = ClientHello, `\x16\x03\x03` = ServerHello) followed by encrypted application data (`\x17\x03\x03`). This is the actual AirDrop file transfer happening over an encrypted channel. There are also a few retransmitted packets (pkts 35-37 are identical), suggesting minor network hiccups.

4. **Three devices involved:** Based on the IPv6 link-local addresses and UUIDs, there are at least three devices — `fe80::6caa:9cff:fe6a:18e3`, `fe80::f80f:9fff:fe6b:475c` (UUID `310e2044-...`), and `fe80::c094:6aff:fe99:1904` (UUID `63288d94-...`).

**Anything concerning?** Not really — this is completely normal Apple ecosystem traffic you'd see on any local Wi-Fi network when someone uses AirDrop. The only mildly interesting detail is that the device name leaks that it's a German-language iPhone ("iPhone von"), and the AirDrop authentication token `607acdd2439e` is visible in the mDNS announcements, which is expected behavior for the protocol.

input.command:
python3 << 'PYEOF'
from scapy.all import *

packets = rdpcap("/mnt/user-data/uploads/test-capture.pcapng")
print(f"Total packets: {len(packets)}")
print()

# Protocol breakdown
from collections import Counter
protos = Counter()
src_ips = Counter()
dst_ips = Counter()
src_ports = Counter()
dst_ports = Counter()
dns_queries = []
http_payloads = []

for p in packets:
    # Layer breakdown
    layers = []
    layer = p
    while layer:
        layers.append(layer.__class__.__name__)
        layer = layer.payload if layer.payload and layer.payload.__class__.__name__ != "NoPayload" else None
    protos[" / ".join(layers)] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(IP):
        src_ips[p[IP].src] += 1
        dst_ips[p[IP].dst] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(TCP):
        src_ports[p[TCP].sport] += 1
        dst_ports[p[TCP].dport] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(UDP):
        src_ports[p[UDP].sport] += 1
        dst_ports[p[UDP].dport] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(DNS) and p[DNS].qr == 0:  # DNS query
        for i in range(p[DNS].qdcount):
            dns_queries.append(p[DNS].qd[i].qname.decode())
    
    # Check for raw payload / HTTP
    if p.haslayer(Raw):
        payload = bytes(p[Raw].load)
        try:
            text = payload.decode('utf-8', errors='replace')
            if any(text.startswith(m) for m in ['GET ', 'POST ', 'HTTP/', 'PUT ', 'DELETE ', 'HEAD ']):
                http_payloads.append(text[:200])
        except:
            pass

print("=== Protocol Breakdown ===")
for proto, count in protos.most_common(20):
    print(f"  {proto}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== Top Source IPs ===")
for ip, count in src_ips.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {ip}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== Top Destination IPs ===")
for ip, count in dst_ips.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {ip}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== Top Destination Ports ===")
for port, count in dst_ports.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {port}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== DNS Queries ({len(dns_queries)}) ===")
for q in dns_queries[:20]:
    print(f"  {q}")

print(f"\n=== HTTP Payloads ({len(http_payloads)}) ===")
for h in http_payloads[:10]:
    print(f"  {h[:150]}")

display_content.json_block:
{"language": "bash", "code": "python3 << 'PYEOF'
from scapy.all import *

packets = rdpcap("/mnt/user-data/uploads/test-capture.pcapng")
print(f"Total packets: {len(packets)}")
print()

# Protocol breakdown
from collections import Counter
protos = Counter()
src_ips = Counter()
dst_ips = Counter()
src_ports = Counter()
dst_ports = Counter()
dns_queries = []
http_payloads = []

for p in packets:
    # Layer breakdown
    layers = []
    layer = p
    while layer:
        layers.append(layer.__class__.__name__)
        layer = layer.payload if layer.payload and layer.payload.__class__.__name__ != "NoPayload" else None
    protos[" / ".join(layers)] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(IP):
        src_ips[p[IP].src] += 1
        dst_ips[p[IP].dst] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(TCP):
        src_ports[p[TCP].sport] += 1
        dst_ports[p[TCP].dport] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(UDP):
        src_ports[p[UDP].sport] += 1
        dst_ports[p[UDP].dport] += 1
    
    if p.haslayer(DNS) and p[DNS].qr == 0:  # DNS query
        for i in range(p[DNS].qdcount):
            dns_queries.append(p[DNS].qd[i].qname.decode())
    
    # Check for raw payload / HTTP
    if p.haslayer(Raw):
        payload = bytes(p[Raw].load)
        try:
            text = payload.decode('utf-8', errors='replace')
            if any(text.startswith(m) for m in ['GET ', 'POST ', 'HTTP/', 'PUT ', 'DELETE ', 'HEAD ']):
                http_payloads.append(text[:200])
        except:
            pass

print("=== Protocol Breakdown ===")
for proto, count in protos.most_common(20):
    print(f"  {proto}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== Top Source IPs ===")
for ip, count in src_ips.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {ip}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== Top Destination IPs ===")
for ip, count in dst_ips.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {ip}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== Top Destination Ports ===")
for port, count in dst_ports.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {port}: {count}")

print(f"\n=== DNS Queries ({len(dns_queries)}) ===")
for q in dns_queries[:20]:
    print(f"  {q}")

print(f"\n=== HTTP Payloads ({len(http_payloads)}) ===")
for h in http_payloads[:10]:
    print(f"  {h[:150]}")
    print("  ---")

PYEOF"}

input.command:
python3 << 'PYEOF'
from scapy.all import *

packets = rdpcap("/mnt/user-data/uploads/test-capture.pcapng")

# IPv6 analysis
from collections import Counter
src_ipv6 = Counter()
dst_ipv6 = Counter()

for p in packets:
    if p.haslayer(IPv6):
        src_ipv6[p[IPv6].src] += 1
        dst_ipv6[p[IPv6].dst] += 1

print("=== Source IPv6 ===")
for ip, c in src_ipv6.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {ip}: {c}")

print("\n=== Dest IPv6 ===")
for ip, c in dst_ipv6.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {ip}: {c}")

# DNS details
print("\n=== All DNS queries with details ===")
for i, p in enumerate(packets):
    if p.haslayer(DNS):
        dns = p[DNS]
        qr = "Response" if dns.qr else "Query"
        if dns.qr == 1 and dns.ancount > 0:
            print(f"Pkt {i}: DNS {qr}, answers:")
            for j in range(dns.ancount):
                ans = dns.an[j] if hasattr(dns.an, '__getitem__') else dns.an
                print(f"    {ans.rrname.decode()} -> type={ans.type}, rdata={ans.rdata if hasattr(ans,'rdata') else 'N/A'}")
                if j > 5: break

# TCP stream content
print("\n=== TCP Raw Payloads ===")
for i, p in enumerate(packets):
    if p.haslayer(Raw) and p.haslayer(TCP):
        payload = bytes(p[Raw].load)
        print(f"Pkt {i} ({p[TCP].sport} -> {p[TCP].dport}): {payload[:100]}")

# Timestamps
print(f"\n=== Time Range ===")
print(f"First packet: {float(packets[0].time)}")
print(f"Last packet: {float(packets[-1].time)}")
print(f"Duration: {float(packets[-1].time) - float(packets[0].time):.2f} seconds")

# Ethernet
print("\n=== MAC Addresses ===")
macs = Counter()
for p in packets:
    if p.haslayer(Ether):
        macs[f"src={p[Ether].src}"] += 1
        macs[f"dst={p[Ether].dst}"] += 1
for m, c in macs.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {m}: {c}")

PYEOF

content.text:
Here's what's going on in this capture — it's an **AirDrop session** between Apple devices on a local network. Here's the breakdown:

**The basics:** 78 packets over about 3.75 minutes, entirely IPv6 over link-local addresses. No internet traffic at all — this is purely device-to-device communication.

**What's happening:**

1. **mDNS service discovery (53% of traffic):** 41 packets are multicast DNS (port 5353, destination `ff02::fb`) — this is Bonjour/mDNS, Apple's local service discovery protocol. The devices are advertising and querying for services like `_airdrop._tcp.local`, `_airplay-p2p._tcp.local`, `_raop._tcp.local`, and `_applicationServicePairing._tcp.local`.

2. **An AirDrop transfer:** Two AirDrop-capable devices discover each other. One device advertises itself as instance `42cb7bc076b6`, another as `f078b69be0bc`. There's also an **iPhone** involved — its service name shows up as **"iPhone von "** (which is German for "iPhone of", with the owner's name likely truncated or stripped). Its pairing session ID is `CA3F2AFD-677A-4919-881B-587416C66F51`.

3. **TLS handshake + encrypted transfer:** The 37 TCP packets (ports 64114 ↔ 57492) show a TLS 1.3 handshake (`\x16\x03\x01` = ClientHello, `\x16\x03\x03` = ServerHello) followed by encrypted application data (`\x17\x03\x03`). This is the actual AirDrop file transfer happening over an encrypted channel. There are also a few retransmitted packets (pkts 35-37 are identical), suggesting minor network hiccups.

4. **Three devices involved:** Based on the IPv6 link-local addresses and UUIDs, there are at least three devices — `fe80::6caa:9cff:fe6a:18e3`, `fe80::f80f:9fff:fe6b:475c` (UUID `310e2044-...`), and `fe80::c094:6aff:fe99:1904` (UUID `63288d94-...`).

**Anything concerning?** Not really — this is completely normal Apple ecosystem traffic you'd see on any local Wi-Fi network when someone uses AirDrop. The only mildly interesting detail is that the device name leaks that it's a German-language iPhone ("iPhone von"), and the AirDrop authentication token `607acdd2439e` is visible in the mDNS announcements, which is expected behavior for the protocol.

chat_messages.text: how does card payments work on airplanes? cause usually there is no data/internet right?

content.text: how does card payments work on airplanes? cause usually there is no data/internet right?

chat_messages.text:
Great question! The short answer is that the payment terminal on the plane **stores your card details and processes the transaction later** once the plane lands and connects to a network.

Here's how it works: when you swipe or tap your card for an in-flight purchase, the seatback terminal or the crew's handheld device captures your card number, expiry, and authorization details, but it doesn't actually charge you in real time. Instead, it performs what's called a **store-and-forward** transaction — the payment data is saved locally on the device and then batch-processed once the aircraft is back on the ground and the system can connect to the payment network.

To reduce the risk of declined cards, airlines typically use a few safeguards. They may check your card against a locally cached blocklist of known-bad card numbers (downloaded before the flight). Some systems do a basic offline authorization using the chip on your card — EMV chips can approve small transactions offline based on risk parameters set by your bank, without needing to phone home. This is why you'll sometimes see airlines only accept chip or contactless cards rather than magnetic stripe.

The tradeoff is that airlines accept some fraud risk — since they can't verify funds in real time, a card could technically be maxed out or cancelled and they wouldn't know until landing. That's one reason in-flight prices tend to be marked up and why some airlines set a minimum or maximum purchase amount.

Some newer aircraft do have satellite-based internet (the same systems that provide onboard Wi-Fi), and a few airlines are starting to use that for real-time payment processing, but store-and-forward is still the dominant approach.

name: Malta 3-day itinerary with Gozo and Comino boat tour
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

The person is planning a 3-day, 2-night trip to Malta from May 2–4 and asked Claude to draft a full itinerary. They are staying in Wied il-Għajn (Marsaskala area) and had one pre-booked commitment: a full-day boat tour on May 3 covering Gozo, Comino, the Blue Lagoon, and sea caves, with an 8:30 AM arrival requirement at the departure point in Buġibba.

Claude built a day-by-day itinerary structured around the accommodation location and the fixed boat tour constraint. Day 1 (Saturday, May 2) focused on Valletta — Upper Barrakka Gardens, St. John's Co-Cathedral, Republic Street, and a ferry crossing to the Three Cities — with dinner at Tal-Familja near the accommodation. Day 2 (Sunday, May 3) was dedicated to the boat tour, with a departure time warning of 7:45 AM from Marsaskala and a relaxed waterfront dinner at Taninu Bistro afterward. Day 3 (Monday, May 4) covered Marsaxlokk harbour, the Blue Grotto sea caves, and Mdina, with an optional farewell lunch stop in Valletta. Claude noted practical details throughout: online booking recommendations, cash requirements, opening day considerations for specific attractions, approximate drive times from the accommodation, and approximate costs.

**Tool Knowledge**

Claude used a places search tool with multiple simultaneous queries covering attractions, restaurants, and departure logistics across different areas of Malta. Batching queries by category and location (e.g., separating Valletta attractions, Marsaskala dining, and Buġibba departure logistics into distinct queries) proved effective for building a geographically coherent itinerary. The map display tool was used with driving as the travel mode and route display enabled, structured across three labeled days with per-location arrival times, duration estimates, and practical notes embedded in each pin.

Here's your full Malta itinerary. The weather looks great — around 18–20°C and dry all three days.

**Day 1 (Sat, May 2) — Valletta & the Three Cities.** Start at Upper Barrakka Gardens for the best harbour panorama (the noon cannon salute is a fun bonus). Walk to St. John's Co-Cathedral — book tickets online to skip the queue; the Caravaggio paintings inside are jaw-dropping. Grab lunch at TRiBE on South Street, then stroll Republic Street (pop into Casa Rocca Piccola if you're curious about Maltese noble life). Take the ferry across to Birgu (€4.50 return, 10 min) — it's quieter and more atmospheric than Valletta, and the waterfront walk to Senglea's vedette lookout is lovely. Head back for dinner at **Tal-Familja**, which is just minutes from your accommodation — traditional Maltese food with a great wine list.

**Day 2 (Sun, May 3) — Gozo, Comino & Blue Lagoon boat trip.** Leave Marsaskala by 7:45 AM at the latest — it's roughly a 35-minute drive to the Buġibba Jetty. Bring sunscreen, swimwear, a towel, and a light layer for the boat. Tours typically return around 5–6 PM. After a long day on the water, keep dinner simple at **Taninu Bistro** on the Marsaskala waterfront, walking distance from your place.

**Day 3 (Mon, May 4) — Marsaxlokk, Blue Grotto & Mdina.** Start early at Marsaxlokk (only 10 min from your place) — while the famous Sunday market is the day before, the colourful luzzu boats and harbourfront pastizzi shops are just as charming on a Monday morning. Then drive 25 min to the **Blue Grotto** for a €10 boat tour through the sea caves (cash only, ~20 min — morning light is best). After that, head inland to **Mdina**, the ancient walled "Silent City" — car-free, golden stone, with rampart views across the whole island. If you have time on the way to the airport or back, stop in Valletta for a farewell meal at **Angela's** — a family-run spot with recipes going back five generations.

A few practical tips: driving is the easiest way to get around (Bolt works well too), St. John's Co-Cathedral is closed Sundays so Saturday is the right day for it, and bring cash for the Blue Grotto boats. Have a wonderful trip!

Mdina: A Local's Guide to Visiting Malta's Silent City
Mdina, known as Malta's "Silent City" and formerly its capital (until 1530), is a 4,000-year-old fortified medieval town perched on a hilltop in the island's center. This UNESCO-recognized,car-free city features a unique mix of medieval and Baroque architecture, with a population of only about 250–300, making it a quiet,, romantic, and popular historical destination.
"The Silent City" nickname stems from its quiet, almost empty atmosphere ...", "A major highlight of Malta.  Mdina is relatively flat and small,  so you won't get lost. Skip the horse ride carriage.
Exuberant palaces, elaborate doors and knockers, residential area with multiple shops.", "Visiting Mdina, the old capital of Malta, was a wonderful experience. With its historic churches, monasteries, and convents, Mdina felt even more charming than Valletta to me.", "It’s simply incredible to touch centuries-old history with your own hands and feel the spirit of this legendary place.

Great weather ahead — no rain expected. Here's your itinerary:

input.title: Malta: 3 Days, 2 Nights
input.narrative: A packed but relaxed trip mixing Valletta's history, a full-day boat adventure to Gozo and Comino, and Malta's stunning southern coast — all with great food near your home base in Marsaskala.

days.title: Valletta & the Three Cities
days.narrative: Hit Malta's capital for its golden-stone streets, baroque masterpieces, and harbour views, then ferry across to the atmospheric Three Cities before dinner near your accommodation.

locations.name: Upper Barrakka Gardens
locations.notes: Arrive early to beat the crowds. Cannon salute fires at noon — worth timing your visit around it. Best panoramic views of the Grand Harbour.

locations.name: St. John's Co-Cathedral
locations.notes: Book tickets online to skip the queue. The Caravaggio paintings alone are worth the visit. Modest dress required.

locations.name: TRiBE Valletta (Lunch)
locations.notes: Great brunch-style spot on South Street. Try their open sourdough dishes.

locations.name: Republic Street & Valletta Stroll
locations.notes: Walk Republic Street end to end — pop into Casa Rocca Piccola if you're curious about Maltese noble life (€9.50, guided tours available).

locations.name: Ferry to Three Cities
locations.notes: €4.50 return. A scenic 10-minute crossing — feels like a mini cruise.

locations.name: Birgu Waterfront
locations.notes: Wander the narrow streets of Birgu (Vittoriosa) — quieter and more atmospheric than Valletta. Walk along the waterfront and over to Senglea for the vedette lookout point.

locations.name: Tal-Familja Restaurant (Dinner)
locations.notes: Just minutes from your accommodation. Traditional Maltese food, excellent wine list, great value. Try the rabbit if it's on the menu. Closed Mondays but you're here on a Saturday!

days.title: Gozo, Comino & Blue Lagoon Boat Trip
days.narrative: Your pre-booked boat tour day. You need to be at the departure point in Buġibba by 8:30 AM — that's about a 35-minute drive from Marsaskala, so leave by 7:45 AM at the latest.

locations.name: Buġibba Jetty (Boat Departure)
locations.notes: Arrive by 8:30 AM. The drive from Marsaskala is ~35 min. Bring sunscreen, swimwear, towel, and a light jacket for the boat. Tours typically return around 5–6 PM.

locations.name: Taninu Bistro (Dinner)
locations.notes: Right on the Marsaskala waterfront, a short walk from your place. Perfect for a relaxed dinner after a long day at sea. Try the Pili Pili Prawns.

days.title: Marsaxlokk, Blue Grotto & Mdina
days.narrative: Your final day mixes the colourful fishing village of Marsaxlokk (Sunday market!), the stunning Blue Grotto caves, and the medieval walled city of Mdina — the 'Silent City.'

locations.name: Marsaxlokk Sunday Market
locations.notes: Only 10 min from your place! The famous Sunday fish market is a must — colourful luzzu boats line the harbour. Grab a pastizzi and fresh fish for breakfast. Get there early (by 9 AM) before the tour buses.

locations.name: Blue Grotto
locations.notes: About 25 min drive from Marsaxlokk. The boat tour through the sea caves costs €10 (cash only) and lasts ~20 min. Morning light makes the water glow the deepest blue. Check it's open — closed Thursdays & Fridays, but you're here Monday!

locations.name: Mdina Gate
locations.notes: The ancient walled capital — car-free, golden stone, and stunning views. Walk the ramparts, peek into St. Paul's Cathedral, and get lost in the quiet alleys. A Game of Thrones filming location!

locations.name: Angela's Valletta (Farewell Lunch)
locations.notes: If you have time on the way back, stop in Valletta for a final Maltese meal. Family-run, traditional recipes going back 5 generations. Try the braġioli (beef roulades) or octopus stew.

Here's your full Malta itinerary. The weather looks great — around 18–20°C and dry all three days.

**Day 1 (Sat, May 2) — Valletta & the Three Cities.** Start at Upper Barrakka Gardens for the best harbour panorama (the noon cannon salute is a fun bonus). Walk to St. John's Co-Cathedral — book tickets online to skip the queue; the Caravaggio paintings inside are jaw-dropping. Grab lunch at TRiBE on South Street, then stroll Republic Street (pop into Casa Rocca Piccola if you're curious about Maltese noble life). Take the ferry across to Birgu (€4.50 return, 10 min) — it's quieter and more atmospheric than Valletta, and the waterfront walk to Senglea's vedette lookout is lovely. Head back for dinner at **Tal-Familja**, which is just minutes from your accommodation — traditional Maltese food with a great wine list.

**Day 2 (Sun, May 3) — Gozo, Comino & Blue Lagoon boat trip.** Leave Marsaskala by 7:45 AM at the latest — it's roughly a 35-minute drive to the Buġibba Jetty. Bring sunscreen, swimwear, a towel, and a light layer for the boat. Tours typically return around 5–6 PM. After a long day on the water, keep dinner simple at **Taninu Bistro** on the Marsaskala waterfront, walking distance from your place.

**Day 3 (Mon, May 4) — Marsaxlokk, Blue Grotto & Mdina.** Start early at Marsaxlokk (only 10 min from your place) — while the famous Sunday market is the day before, the colourful luzzu boats and harbourfront pastizzi shops are just as charming on a Monday morning. Then drive 25 min to the **Blue Grotto** for a €10 boat tour through the sea caves (cash only, ~20 min — morning light is best). After that, head inland to **Mdina**, the ancient walled "Silent City" — car-free, golden stone, with rampart views across the whole island. If you have time on the way to the airport or back, stop in Valletta for a farewell meal at **Angela's** — a family-run spot with recipes going back five generations.

A few practical tips: driving is the easiest way to get around (Bolt works well too), St. John's Co-Cathedral is closed Sundays so Saturday is the right day for it, and bring cash for the Blue Grotto boats. Have a wonderful trip!
